summary:
As of March 25, 2026, the Canadian government bond market is navigating a period of intense volatility and a significant "bear-flattening" of the yield curve. Following the Bank of Canada’s and the Federal Reserve’s respective decisions on March 18 to hold policy rates steady (at 2.25% and 3.50–3.75%), yields have spiked across the board. This move was not driven by central bank hawkishness—both banks signaled a "wait-and-see" approach—but rather by a massive geopolitical risk premium. The escalation of conflict in the Middle East earlier this month has sent WTI oil prices toward $100/bbl, effectively "staling" February’s promising 1.8% headline inflation print and forcing markets to price in a "higher-for-longer" scenario to combat looming energy-driven price shocks. 

For our institution, the data reveals a sharp upward shift in funding costs. The 2-year yield has surged nearly 50 basis points from its February lows, peaking at 3.07% on March 20, while the 5-year yield climbed to 3.20%. Crucially, the 2Y-5Y spread has compressed from a healthy 36bps in early February to a tight 19bps as of March 23. This flattening curve directly pressures our Net Interest Margin (NIM); as short-term yields (which benchmark our GICs and wholesale funding) rise faster than the 5-year yields (which benchmark our fixed-rate mortgages), our cost of funds is escalating more rapidly than the yields on our primary lending assets. To maintain profitability, we must immediately adjust our 5-year fixed mortgage rates upward to mirror the 3.14%+ 5Y bond floor, while remaining aggressive on GIC pricing to retain liquidity in this high-rate environment.

The strong correlation with US Treasuries remains a dominant factor. The US 10-year yield recently hit 4.39%, its highest level since mid-2025, pulling Canadian yields higher in its wake despite Canada’s weaker domestic growth and recent employment losses. For a small FI, this means our domestic lending environment is currently being dictated by global energy markets and US fiscal dynamics rather than purely Canadian economic fundamentals. We anticipate continued pressure on lending margins through Q2 2026 as we navigate this "oil-shock" volatility, requiring a disciplined approach to spread management and a cautious outlook on credit growth as higher borrowing costs begin to weigh on already fragile consumer sentiment.
